misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand why multiplying both dividend and divisor by the same number leaves the quotient unchanged.

visuals.ruleText: Division is a ratio — scaling both parts equally leaves the ratio unchanged
visuals.subText: 4.8 ÷ 0.6 and 48 ÷ 6 are the same ratio — just expressed differently

steps.narration: Division measures how many times one quantity fits into another — it is a ratio. If you scale both quantities by the same factor, the ratio is unchanged. Four point eight and zero point six, each multiplied by ten, become forty-eight and six — the same ratio, easier to compute.

lines.text: = 8  ✓  (same as multiplying num and denom by 10)

steps.narration: Write the division as a fraction. Multiplying numerator and denominator by ten is the same as multiplying a fraction by ten over ten — which equals one. Multiplying by one never changes the value.

How many sixty-pence portions fit into four pounds eighty? Converting both to pence: four hundred and eighty pence divided by sixty pence equals eight portions. Same quantities, expressed in a different unit — the ratio is unchanged.

visuals.label: Ratio unchanged by scaling — multiply both by 10 to work with whole numbers

steps.narration: Eight. Scaling both values by ten is like changing units — the ratio, and therefore the answer, stays the same.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: worrying that multiplying changes the answer. It doesn't — division is a ratio, and scaling both parts equally preserves the ratio. 4.8 ÷ 0.6 = 48 ÷ 6 = 8.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Division is a ratio. Multiplying both the dividend and divisor by the same number is like changing units — the ratio stays the same, so the answer is unchanged.
